title: Cat Yellow Eye Discharge: Causes, Urgency, and Care Guide
meta_desc: Noticed yellow discharge from your cat's eyes? Learn what causes it, why it is medium urgency, and how to help your feline friend today.
content_html:
TL;DR: Yellow or green eye discharge in cats typically indicates a bacterial or viral infection and should be evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. Providing a clear photo of the eyes can help a specialist determine if immediate urgent care is necessary.

What causes yellow or greenish eye discharge in my cat?

Yellow or greenish discharge from a cat's eyes is typically a sign of an infection or inflammation. While clear tearing can sometimes be caused by allergies or dust, thick colored discharge usually indicates that the immune system is responding to a bacterial or viral pathogen, such as Feline Herpesvirus or Calicivirus. It may also be related to conjunctivitis or an underlying respiratory issue.

Is yellow eye discharge in cats considered a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for yellow discharge from both eyes is Medium.
  • This is generally not a life-threatening emergency unless your cat is having difficulty breathing or is completely lethargic.
  • Eye issues can progress rapidly and become painful, so you should aim to schedule a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Early treatment helps prevent potential damage to the corneas or chronic respiratory complications.

How can a photo help my vet triage my cat's eye issue?

  • Taking a clear, well-lit photo of your cat's eyes is an invaluable tool for your veterinarian or a triage specialist.
  • A photo allows a professional to see the exact color and thickness of the discharge.
  • Visuals help the vet look for signs of swelling (chemosis) and determine if the third eyelid is elevated.
  • This information helps determine if your cat needs an immediate urgent care visit or if a standard appointment will suffice.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, yellow eye discharge is often associated with infectious diseases such as feline herpesvirus 1, Chlamydia, and Mycoplasma, frequently presenting as acute and recurrent conjunctivitis. These pathogens can also cause ulcerative and stromal keratitis, proliferative keratoconjunctivitis, corneal sequestrum, corneal symblepharon, and keratoconjunctivitis sicca. Systemic diseases like feline infectious peritonitis, toxoplasmosis, feline immunodeficiency virus, and feline leukemia virus can manifest as anterior and posterior uveitis, chronic uveitis, retinal detachment, and secondary glaucoma. Cytologic examination of Giemsa-stained conjunctival scrapings is valuable for identifying Chlamydia and Mycoplasma, and PCR of ocular, nasal, or caudal pharyngeal secretions may help establish a definitive diagnosis. Treatment is largely symptomatic and supportive, with broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., amoxicillin with clavulanic acid, cephalosporins, trimethoprim-sulfa, fluoroquinolones, tetracyclines) used against secondary bacterial infections.

Chapter: Infectious Disease, Ophthalmology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1482)

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops for my cat?
faqs.answer: No. Never use human over-the-counter eye drops on a cat, as some ingredients can be toxic or cause severe irritation. Only use medications prescribed by a veterinarian.

faqs.question: How can I make my cat more comfortable while waiting for the vet?
faqs.answer: You can gently wipe away the crust and discharge using a soft cloth or cotton ball dampened with lukewarm water. Use a fresh surface for each eye to avoid spreading bacteria.

faqs.question: Is this condition contagious to other cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, many causes of yellow eye discharge, such as feline respiratory infections, are highly contagious. It is best to keep the affected cat separated from other pets until they have been evaluated.
